skill.name: routeros-netinstall
skill.description: MikroTik netinstall-cli for automated RouterOS device flashing. Use when: automating netinstall, writing scripts that invoke netinstall-cli, building netinstall tooling, understanding etherboot/BOOTP/TFTP protocols, working with RouterOS package files (.npk), using modescript or configure script, or when the user mentions netinstall, etherboot, or device flashing.
skill.summary: Documentation for MikroTik netinstall-cli tool covering automated RouterOS device flashing, etherboot mode, configure/mode scripts, and multi-device provisioning.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation/information skill providing guidance on MikroTik netinstall-cli usage. All 113 static analyzer flags are false positives or misclassifications. The backtick patterns are Makefile syntax in documentation examples. Sudo usage is legitimate (tool requires root for privileged BOOTP/TFTP ports). No cryptographic algorithms are implemented. The skill poses no security risk to users.

medium_findings.title: Misclassified sudo privilege escalation
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'sudo netinstall-cli' usage as privilege escalation. This is FALSE POSITIVE - the netinstall-cli tool legitimately requires root privileges for BOOTP (ports 67/68) and TFTP (port 69) network operations. Documentation correctly shows proper sudo usage for this sysadmin tool.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: netinstall-cli requires privileged ports - documented requirement, not vulnerability

low_findings.title: Documentation examples containing Makefile syntax
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged '$(shell ...)' as Ruby backtick execution. This is FALSE POSITIVE - lines 144-149 contain Makefile documentation showing version resolution patterns, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Makefile syntax in documentation examples, not actual shell execution

low_findings.title: Hardcoded MikroTik download URLs
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged hardcoded URLs to download.mikrotik.com and upgrade.mikrotik.com. These are legitimate official MikroTik download endpoints for RouterOS packages - not security concerns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Official MikroTik CDN URLs for RouterOS downloads - legitimate infrastructure

low_findings.title: Example IP address in documentation
low_findings.description: Line 169 shows example IP 192.168.88.2/24 for network configuration documentation. Standard practice for documentation - no actual IP scanning or network probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Common RFC 1918 example IP in documentation - standard practice

low_findings.title: Markdown relative path references flagged as path traversal
low_findings.description: Lines 109 and 140 reference '../routeros-fundamentals/references/version-parsing.md' as markdown links to other skill documentation. This is standard cross-referencing, not path traversal vulnerability.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Markdown documentation links between skill files - not executable path traversal

dangerous_patterns.title: Static analyzer misclassified keywords as crypto weakness
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at lines 3, 22, 39, 40, 80, 197, 198, 202. These are FALSE POSITIVES - the analyzer confused documentation keywords ('keyfile', 'license key', 'configure script') with cryptographic implementation. This documentation skill contains NO cryptographic algorithms.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Documentation keywords misidentified as crypto - no actual cryptographic code exists

content.user_title: Install RouterOS firmware with netinstall-cli automation
content.value_statement: Manually flashing MikroTik devices is time-consuming and error-prone. This skill provides Claude Code with comprehensive documentation for automating RouterOS installation using netinstall-cli, including multi-device provisioning and first-boot configuration.
actual_capabilities: Generate netinstall-cli commands for single and multi-device RouterOS installation
Explain etherboot mode entry methods for different MikroTik hardware
Document configure script and mode script usage for first-boot automation
Provide architecture-specific package naming conventions for all RouterOS platforms
Detail network requirements including privileged ports and L2 connectivity
Reference related skills for QEMU emulation and container deployment
limitations: Does not execute netinstall-cli directly - provides documentation only
Requires manual physical access to devices for etherboot mode entry
Does not provide RouterOS package download functionality

use_cases.title: Automate RouterOS installation for network provisioning
use_cases.description: Generate netinstall-cli commands with proper package ordering and sudo privileges for batch device provisioning in warehouse or lab environments.
use_cases.target_user: Network engineers provisioning multiple MikroTik devices

use_cases.title: Configure first-boot settings with mode scripts
use_cases.description: Create modescript.rsc files that enable device-mode, container support, or ZeroTier on first boot without manual power cycling.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot netinstall connectivity issues
use_cases.description: Understand network requirements, etherboot mode detection, and DHCP snooping workarounds for successful device flashing.

prompt_templates.title: Basic single device install
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate netinstall-cli command to install RouterOS 7.22 on a single MikroTik device connected to eth0. Include the system package and container package for ARM64.
prompt_templates.scenario: Single device flashing with basic packages

prompt_templates.title: Multi-device service loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a netinstall-cli command that will continuously flash any MikroTik device that boots into etherboot mode, processing each MAC address only once.
prompt_templates.scenario: Provisioning service for mixed device types

prompt_templates.title: Mode script for container support
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a mode script that enables advanced mode and container support on first boot for RouterOS 7.22+. Show how to pass it to netinstall-cli.

prompt_templates.title: Non-x86 host setup
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I run netinstall-cli on an ARM64 Linux host to flash an ARM64 RouterOS device? What QEMU setup is required?

output_examples.input: Generate netinstall-cli command for single ARM64 device with container support
Create mode script to enable containers on first boot

output_examples.output: netinstall-cli requires root/sudo for privileged ports: BOOTP (67/68) and TFTP (69). The device must be on the same Layer 2 segment.

best_practices: Always list the system package (routeros-VER-ARCH.npk) first in the package list for correct installation order
Use a dedicated network interface or switch without DHCP servers to avoid BOOTP conflicts during netinstall
Write configure scripts to /etc/dhcpd.conf or similar persistent storage, not temporary locations, since they persist across upgrades
anti_patterns: Do not run netinstall-cli without sudo - it requires privileged network ports (67-69) that are inaccessible to non-root users
Do not mix architectures in a single netinstall run - provide packages only for the target device architecture
Do not skip the -r flag if you want a clean install - without -r or -e, netinstall preserves the old configuration which may cause conflicts

faq.question: What is the difference between configure script (-s) and mode script (-sm)?
faq.answer: Configure script (-s) runs after default config is applied and persists across upgrades. Mode script (-sm) runs once on first boot before default config and auto-deletes after execution. Mode script also supports device-mode changes that trigger immediate reboot.

faq.question: Can I run netinstall-cli on ARM64 or ARM hosts?
faq.answer: netinstall-cli is an x86 ELF binary. On non-x86 hosts, use QEMU user-mode emulation (qemu-i386-static) on ARM/ARM64 Linux, or run QEMU system VM on macOS. See the tikoci-qemu-user-emulation skill for setup details.

faq.question: Why is my device not appearing in netinstall?
faq.answer: Ensure the device is in etherboot mode (hold reset during power-on, or press Ctrl+E via serial). Check that the host interface has a static IP in the same subnet. Verify no DHCP server is running on that network segment - BOOTP and DHCP use the same ports and will conflict.

faq.question: What RouterOS package architecture do I need?
faq.answer: RouterOS uses different architectures: ARM, ARM64, MIPSBE, MMIPS, SMIPS, PPC, TILE, and x86. Check your device model on the MikroTik product page to determine the correct architecture. Package names follow the pattern: routeros-VERSION-ARCH.npk

faq.question: How do I preserve my license key during netinstall?
faq.answer: The license key and RouterBOOT settings are automatically preserved during netinstall - you do not need to backup anything. Just run netinstall-cli with your desired packages and configuration.

faq.question: Can I run netinstall inside a RouterOS container?
faq.answer: Yes, on RouterOS 7.21+ you can run netinstall-cli in a container with VETH networking. Configure the container with NETINSTALL environment variables for architecture, packages, channel, and interface. See the routeros-container skill for detailed setup.
